Guideline: Use echo.New() as the single entry point
Description: Create one Echo instance per application and configure it centrally before starting the server
Do: Initialize Echo once and store in a variable for reuse
Dont: Don't create multiple Echo instances or call echo.New() in handlers
Code Good:
e := echo.New()
e.HideBanner = true
e.Server.ReadTimeout = 10 * time.Second

Guideline: Register routes with typed methods e.GET, e.POST
Description: Use the specific HTTP method functions to register handlers for clarity and type safety
Do: Use e.GET e.POST e.PUT e.DELETE e.PATCH for each route
Dont: Don't use e.Add with string method names unless building dynamic routes
Code Good:
e.GET("/users", getUsers)
e.POST("/users", createUser)
e.DELETE("/users/:id", deleteUser)
Code Bad:
e.Add("GET", "/users", getUsers)
e.Add("POST", "/users", createUser)

Guideline: Use e.Group for API versioning and shared middleware
Description: Group related routes under a common prefix and apply middleware at the group level instead of per-route
Do: Create groups for versioned APIs and apply middleware to groups
Dont: Don't repeat prefixes across individual routes or apply the same middleware to every route manually
Code Good:
v1 := e.Group("/api/v1")
v1.Use(middleware.Logger())
v1.GET("/users", getUsers)
v1.POST("/users", createUser)
Code Bad:
e.GET("/api/v1/users", getUsers)
e.POST("/api/v1/users", createUser)
e.GET("/api/v2/users", getUsersV2)

Guideline: Apply middleware in the correct order with e.Use and e.Pre
Description: Use e.Pre for middleware that runs before routing and e.Use for middleware that runs after routing
Do: Use e.Pre for trailing slash removal and e.Use for logging and recovery
Dont: Don't put pre-routing middleware in e.Use or post-routing middleware in e.Pre
Code Good:
e.Pre(middleware.RemoveTrailingSlash())
e.Use(middleware.Logger())
e.Use(middleware.Recover())
Code Bad:
e.Use(middleware.RemoveTrailingSlash())
e.Pre(middleware.Logger())
e.Pre(middleware.Recover())

Guideline: Follow the echo.MiddlewareFunc signature for custom middleware
Description: Return a function that takes echo.HandlerFunc and returns echo.HandlerFunc for composability
Do: Follow the standard middleware pattern with next(c) call chain
Dont: Don't write middleware that skips calling next or breaks the chain unintentionally

Guideline: Use echo.Context methods instead of accessing raw http.Request directly
Description: Echo Context wraps the standard request and response with convenience methods; prefer them for consistency
Do: Use c.Request() c.Response() c.Get() c.Set() for request data
Dont: Don't bypass echo.Context and write to http.ResponseWriter directly
Code Bad:
func handler(c echo.Context) error {
    r := c.Request()
    w := c.Response().Writer
    r.ParseForm()
    w.Write([]byte(r.Form.Get("name")))
    return nil
}

Guideline: Use c.Bind() with struct tags for automatic request parsing
Description: Bind JSON XML form and query parameters to Go structs using appropriate struct tags
Do: Define structs with json query and form tags then call c.Bind
Dont: Don't manually parse request body or read individual form values for structured data

Guideline: Integrate a custom validator using echo.Validator interface
Description: Implement the echo.Validator interface with a library like go-playground/validator for automatic struct validation
Do: Set e.Validator with a custom validator and call c.Validate after c.Bind
Dont: Don't validate manually with if-else chains or skip validation entirely

Guideline: Use c.JSON c.String c.HTML for type-safe responses
Description: Echo provides typed response methods that set Content-Type headers automatically
Do: Use c.JSON for API responses and c.String or c.HTML for simple text
Dont: Don't manually marshal JSON or set Content-Type headers when Echo methods suffice

Guideline: Define a custom HTTPErrorHandler for consistent error responses
Description: Override the default error handler to return structured error responses across all endpoints
Do: Set e.HTTPErrorHandler to a custom function that formats errors consistently
Dont: Don't handle errors differently in each handler or ignore returned errors

Guideline: Use echojwt middleware for token-based authentication
Description: Use the official echo-jwt module for JWT parsing and validation instead of manual token handling
Do: Import github.com/labstack/echo-jwt/v4 and configure with echojwt.Config
Dont: Don't parse JWT tokens manually or store secrets in source code

Guideline: Configure CORS with middleware.CORSWithConfig for explicit origins
Description: Set allowed origins methods and headers explicitly instead of using a wildcard in production
Do: Specify exact allowed origins and methods in CORSConfig
Dont: Don't allow all origins with * in production environments

Guideline: Use middleware.LoggerWithConfig for structured request logging
Description: Configure the logger middleware with a custom format to capture relevant request metadata
Do: Use LoggerWithConfig with a structured output format including latency and status
Dont: Don't rely on fmt.Println or log.Println scattered in handlers for request logging

Guideline: Always use middleware.Recover to prevent server crashes
Description: The Recover middleware catches panics in the handler chain and returns a 500 error instead of crashing
Do: Add middleware.Recover() as one of the first middleware in the chain
Dont: Don't skip recovery middleware or add it after other middleware that might panic
Code Good:
e.Use(middleware.Recover())
e.Use(middleware.Logger())
e.GET("/users", getUsers)
Code Bad:
e.GET("/users", getUsers)
// no recover middleware - a panic in getUsers crashes the server

Guideline: Use middleware.RateLimiter with a memory store to prevent abuse
Description: Apply rate limiting middleware to protect endpoints from excessive requests
Do: Configure RateLimiter with RateLimiterMemoryStore and appropriate rate values
Dont: Don't leave public APIs unprotected or implement custom rate limiting with global variables
Code Good: e.Use(middleware.RateLimiter(middleware.NewRateLimiterMemoryStore(20)))

Guideline: Use e.Static and e.File for serving static assets
Description: Serve static files from a directory or individual files using Echo built-in methods
Do: Use e.Static for directories and e.File for individual files like favicon
Dont: Don't implement custom handlers to read and serve static files manually
Code Good:
e.Static("/static", "assets")
e.File("/favicon.ico", "assets/favicon.ico")
e.File("/robots.txt", "assets/robots.txt")

Guideline: Implement echo.Renderer interface for HTML template rendering
Description: Create a custom renderer that wraps html/template and register it with e.Renderer
Do: Implement the Render method and use c.Render in handlers
Dont: Don't execute templates manually and write HTML bytes to the response

Guideline: Use c.FormFile to handle file uploads safely
Description: Use Echo built-in c.FormFile to retrieve uploaded files and validate size and type before saving
Do: Use c.FormFile and validate the file before calling io.Copy to save
Dont: Don't parse multipart forms manually or skip file size and type validation

Guideline: Use gorilla/websocket upgrader with Echo handlers
Description: Integrate gorilla/websocket by upgrading the connection inside an Echo handler function
Do: Create a websocket.Upgrader and call Upgrade inside an Echo handler
Dont: Don't mix Echo response methods with raw WebSocket writes or skip origin checking

Guideline: Use e.Shutdown with context for graceful server shutdown
Description: Start the server in a goroutine and use signal.Notify with context to gracefully drain connections
Do: Use context.WithTimeout and e.Shutdown for clean shutdown on interrupt
Dont: Don't call e.Start on the main goroutine without a shutdown mechanism or use log.Fatal(e.Start())

Guideline: Use httptest.NewRequest and httptest.NewRecorder with Echo
Description: Create test cases using net/http/httptest to test Echo handlers without starting a real server
Do: Create an Echo instance and use echo.NewContext with httptest objects
Dont: Don't start a real server in tests or test handlers by calling them as plain functions

Guideline: Name routes with .Name for reverse URL generation
Description: Assign names to routes to enable URL generation using echo.Reverse which avoids hardcoded paths
Do: Call .Name on route registration and use echo.Reverse to build URLs
Dont: Don't hardcode URL paths in templates or redirects when routes could change

Guideline: Use c.Param for path params and c.QueryParam for query strings
Description: Distinguish between path parameters defined in the route and query parameters appended to the URL
Do: Use c.Param for :id style path segments and c.QueryParam for ?key=val
Dont: Don't mix up c.Param and c.QueryParam or access params from the raw URL

Guideline: Use middleware.TimeoutWithConfig to limit handler execution time
Description: Apply timeout middleware to prevent long-running handlers from consuming server resources indefinitely
Do: Configure TimeoutWithConfig with a reasonable duration and custom error message
Dont: Don't let handlers run indefinitely or implement manual timeout logic with goroutines

Guideline: Set HideBanner and HidePort and configure timeouts for production
Description: Disable the startup banner and port log in production and set appropriate server timeouts
Do: Set HideBanner HidePort to true and configure ReadTimeout WriteTimeout on the server
Dont: Don't leave default settings in production or skip server timeout configuration
Code Good:
e := echo.New()
e.HideBanner = true
e.HidePort = true
e.Server.ReadTimeout = 10 * time.Second
e.Server.WriteTimeout = 10 * time.Second
e.Server.IdleTimeout = 30 * time.Second
